questions.text:
Which of the following statements is INCORRECT?
A. There is a repulsive force when north poles (or south poles) of two magnets are brought close together.
B. If a bar magnet is broken into two halves, we get two similar bar magnets with stronger properties.
C. The earth behaves as a magnet with the magnetic field pointing approximately from the geographic south to the north.
D. When a bar magnet is freely suspended, it points in the north-south direction.
options: There is a repulsive force when north poles (or south poles) of two magnets are brought close together.
If a bar magnet is broken into two halves, we get two similar bar magnets with stronger properties.
The earth behaves as a magnet with the magnetic field pointing approximately from the geographic south to the north.
When a bar magnet is freely suspended, it points in the north-south direction.
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (B)

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 13 — Magnetic Effects of Electric Current
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

Statement (B) is INCORRECT because when a bar magnet is broken into two halves, each half becomes a new but WEAKER magnet — not a stronger one.

The magnetic moment of the original magnet:
\( M = m \times 2l \)

After breaking, each half has half the magnetic length:
\( M_{half} = m \times l = \frac{M}{2} \)

So magnetic moment is HALVED → properties become WEAKER, not stronger. Each piece does get its own north and south pole (monopoles cannot exist), making two complete but weaker magnets.

---

(A) Repulsive force between like poles — CORRECT ✓
Like poles (N-N or S-S) always repel. Fundamental magnetic property.

(B) Broken magnet has stronger properties — INCORRECT ✗
Each half has half the magnetic moment → WEAKER properties. This is the answer.

(C) Earth's magnetic field from geographic south to north — CORRECT ✓
Earth's geographic north pole corresponds to its magnetic south pole. So field lines run from geographic south to north, which is why the north-seeking pole of a compass points toward geographic north.

(D) Freely suspended bar magnet points north-south — CORRECT ✓
A freely suspended bar magnet aligns with Earth's magnetic field, pointing approximately north-south. This is the basic compass principle.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Magnetic properties are fundamental to DRDO's NPOL (Naval Physical and Oceanographic Laboratory) torpedo guidance, magnetic mine detection, and electromagnetic shielding in submarines.

questions.text: Which of the following is the number of oscillations per unit time of the sound wave?
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (D) Frequency

📚 NCERT: Class 9 Science | Chapter 12 — Sound
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

Definitions of all four options:

(A) Wavelength (\( \lambda \)): Distance between two consecutive crests or troughs. Unit: metre (m).

(B) Time period (T): Time taken for ONE complete oscillation. Related to frequency by:
\( T = \frac{1}{f} \)

(C) Amplitude (A): Maximum displacement of a particle from its mean position. Determines the loudness/intensity of sound.

(D) Frequency (f) — CORRECT:
Frequency = number of complete oscillations per unit time.
\( f = \frac{\text{Number of oscillations}}{\text{Time}} \)
Unit: Hertz (Hz) = 1 oscillation per second.

Wave equation: \( v = f \times \lambda \)

Sound frequency ranges:
- Infrasound: \( f < 20 \) Hz
- Audible: \( 20 \leq f \leq 20{,}000 \) Hz
- Ultrasound: \( f > 20{,}000 \) Hz

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Frequency analysis is fundamental to DRDO's SONAR systems, ultrasonic NDT of materials at DMRL, and acoustic sensors in submarines.

questions.text: The tissue providing nutrition to the embryo in the uterus is the ________.
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (C) Placenta

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 8 — How Do Organisms Reproduce?
⭐ Exam Relevance: High

The placenta is the specialised organ that develops in the uterus during pregnancy. It serves as the complete interface between mother and embryo.

Functions of placenta:
1. Provides oxygen and nutrients from mother's blood to embryo
2. Removes CO₂ and metabolic wastes from embryo to mother's blood
3. Produces hormones (hCG, progesterone, estrogen) to maintain pregnancy
4. Acts as a partial barrier against pathogens

Connected to embryo via the umbilical cord.

---

(A) Fallopian tube — INCORRECT:
Site of fertilisation (sperm meets egg). Transports fertilised egg to uterus. Does NOT provide nutrition.

(B) Oviduct — INCORRECT:
Another name for Fallopian tube. Same explanation as (A).

(C) Placenta — CORRECT ✓
The specific tissue providing nutrition, oxygen, and waste removal for the embryo.

(D) Villi — INCORRECT:
Villi are finger-like projections ON the placenta that increase surface area for exchange. They are a structural component OF the placenta, not a separate tissue.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: DIPAS (Defence Institute of Physiology and Allied Sciences) studies maternal physiology for soldiers' families in extreme environments.

questions.text: ______ compound is used as a drying agent.
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (A) Calcium oxide

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 2 — Acids, Bases and Salts
⭐ Exam Relevance: High

Calcium oxide (CaO), also called Quicklime, is an excellent drying agent because it reacts vigorously and irreversibly with water:

\( \text{CaO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{Heat} \)

This exothermic reaction makes CaO highly effective at absorbing moisture.

---

(A) Calcium oxide (CaO) — CORRECT ✓
Strong drying agent. Also used in cement, steelmaking, and water treatment.

(B) Calcium carbide (CaC₂) — INCORRECT:
Reacts with water to produce acetylene gas:
\( \text{CaC}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_2 \uparrow \)
Destructively reactive with water — NOT a drying agent.

(C) Gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) — INCORRECT:
Already contains water of crystallisation. Used to make Plaster of Paris. Not a drying agent.

(D) Calcium carbonate (CaCO₃) — INCORRECT:
Does not absorb moisture. Used in antacids, cement, and as a filler.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Drying agents like CaO are used in DRDO labs for maintaining moisture-free environments critical for explosive and propellant storage at HEMRL.

questions.text: If the radius of curvature of a concave mirror is 6.2 cm, its focal length is _______ cm.
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (B) 3.1

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 10 — Light: Reflection and Refraction
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

Relationship between focal length (f) and radius of curvature (R):

\( f = \frac{R}{2} \)

The focal point is the midpoint between the pole and the centre of curvature.

Substituting:
\( f = \frac{6.2}{2} = 3.1 \text{ cm} \)

---

(A) 12.4 — INCORRECT: This is \( R \times 2 = 12.4 \). Multiplied instead of divided.

(B) 3.1 — CORRECT ✓: \( f = R/2 = 6.2/2 = 3.1 \) cm.

(C) 2.6 — INCORRECT: No standard formula gives this result.

(D) 6 — INCORRECT: Rounded R to 6 and incorrectly used as f.

Key formula: \( f = \frac{R}{2} \) or equivalently \( R = 2f \)

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Concave mirror optics are used in DRDO's IRDE searchlights, thermal imaging systems, and parabolic antenna reflectors for defence applications.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. Saturated hydrocarbons contain single bonds only.
2. Ethyne is an example of a saturated hydrocarbon.
3. Carbon can form long chain compounds due to catenation.
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (B) 1 and 3 only

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 4 — Carbon and its Compounds
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

Statement 1: "Saturated hydrocarbons contain single bonds only." — TRUE ✓
Saturated hydrocarbons (alkanes) contain only C-C single bonds. General formula: \( C_nH_{2n+2} \)
Examples: Methane (CH₄), Ethane (C₂H₆). Called 'saturated' because carbons hold maximum hydrogen.

Statement 2: "Ethyne is an example of a saturated hydrocarbon." — FALSE ✗
Ethyne (C₂H₂ / Acetylene) contains a TRIPLE bond:
\( \text{H} - \text{C} \equiv \text{C} - \text{H} \)
Ethyne is an UNSATURATED hydrocarbon (alkyne). Unsaturated hydrocarbons have double or triple bonds:
- Alkenes (double bond): e.g., Ethene C₂H₄
- Alkynes (triple bond): e.g., Ethyne C₂H₂

Statement 3: "Carbon can form long chain compounds due to catenation." — TRUE ✓
Catenation is carbon's unique property of forming bonds with other carbon atoms, creating long chains, branched chains, and rings. This is why millions of organic compounds exist.

Only statements 1 and 3 are correct. Answer = (B).

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Carbon chemistry is the basis of DRDO's high-energy materials at HEMRL — explosives like RDX and HMX are carbon-based catenated compounds.

questions.text: _________ compound has -OH as a functional group.
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (D) Alcohol

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 4 — Carbon and its Compounds
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

Functional groups and their compound classes:

| Functional Group | Compound Class |
|---|---|
| -OH (Hydroxyl) | Alcohol |
| -COOH (Carboxyl) | Carboxylic acid |
| -CHO (Aldehyde) | Aldehyde |
| -CO- (Ketone) | Ketone |
| -NH₂ (Amino) | Amine |

---

(A) Carboxylic acid — INCORRECT:
Functional group is -COOH, not simply -OH.

(B) Ethanoic acid — INCORRECT:
Ethanoic acid (CH₃COOH) has -COOH as its functional group, not -OH.

(C) Acetic acid — INCORRECT:
Acetic acid is the common name for ethanoic acid (same compound as B). Functional group = -COOH.

Note: Options B and C are the same compound with different names.

(D) Alcohol — CORRECT ✓
Alcohol has -OH (hydroxyl) group as its characteristic functional group:
- Methanol: CH₃-OH
- Ethanol: C₂H₅-OH
- Propanol: C₃H₇-OH

🔬 DRDO Relevance: -OH functional group is key in propellant chemistry at HEMRL — nitrating cellulose's -OH groups produces nitrocellulose, the primary component of gun propellants.

questions.text: A car of mass m accelerates uniformly from rest under a constant force F. After traveling a distance d, what is the kinetic energy of the car?
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (B) F × d

📚 NCERT: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 6 — Work, Energy and Power
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

Applying the Work-Energy Theorem:
\( W_{net} = \Delta KE = KE_{final} - KE_{initial} \)

Since the car starts from rest: \( KE_{initial} = 0 \)

Only force F acts horizontally:
\( W = F \times d \)

Therefore:
\( KE_{final} = F \times d \)

Verification via kinematics:
From \( v^2 = u^2 + 2as \) with u = 0, s = d, and \( a = F/m \):
\( v^2 = \frac{2Fd}{m} \)
\( KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2 = \frac{1}{2}m \times \frac{2Fd}{m} = F \times d \) ✓

---

(A) (F×d)/2 — INCORRECT: This would apply if starting with some initial KE, not from rest.

(B) F×d — CORRECT ✓: All work by F converts to KE since car starts from rest.

(C) 2×F×d — INCORRECT: No physical justification for doubling.

(D) (F×d)/m — INCORRECT: Dividing by mass gives \( m^2/s^2 \) (units of v²), not KE (Joules = kg·m²/s²). Dimensionally wrong for KE.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Work-Energy Theorem governs muzzle energy calculations at ARDE — propellant gas force F acting over barrel length d determines projectile kinetic energy at muzzle exit.

questions.text: 'Alum' is an example of:
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (D) Double salt

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 2 — Acids, Bases and Salts
⭐ Exam Relevance: High

Alum = Potassium alum = \( \text{KAl(SO}_4)_2 \cdot 12\text{H}_2\text{O} \)
Full name: Potassium aluminium sulfate dodecahydrate

Why it is a Double salt:
A double salt is formed by combination of two different simple salts crystallising together. Alum contains TWO different metal cations (K⁺ and Al³⁺) with common anion (SO₄²⁻):
- Salt 1: K₂SO₄ (Potassium sulfate)
- Salt 2: Al₂(SO₄)₃ (Aluminium sulfate)

In solution, alum completely dissociates into K⁺, Al³⁺, and SO₄²⁻ (distinguishes it from complex salts which retain complex ions in solution).

---

(A) Flake salt — INCORRECT: A physical form of NaCl. Not a chemical classification.

(B) Table salt — INCORRECT: Table salt = NaCl, a simple single salt.

(C) Single salt — INCORRECT: Single salt formed from one acid and one base. Alum has two different metal ions.

(D) Double salt — CORRECT ✓: Alum = K₂SO₄·Al₂(SO₄)₃·24H₂O = double salt. ✓

Uses of alum: Water purification (coagulant), dyeing (mordant), aftershave (astringent).

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Alum is used as a coagulant in DRDO's field water purification units deployed with the Indian Army.

questions.text: Which statement is NOT true for magnetic field lines?
options: The relative strength of the magnetic field is shown by the degree of closeness of the field lines.
No two field lines are found to cross each other.
Inside the magnet, the direction of field lines is from its south pole to its north pole.
Magnetic field lines are not closed curves.
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (D) Magnetic field lines are not closed curves.

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 13 — Magnetic Effects of Electric Current
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

(A) Closeness shows relative strength — CORRECT statement ✓:
Denser field lines = stronger magnetic field. Field lines are closest at the poles (strongest field).

(B) No two field lines cross — CORRECT statement ✓:
If field lines crossed, the field would have two directions at one point — physically impossible. Hence field lines never intersect.

(C) Inside magnet, direction is S to N — CORRECT statement ✓:
Outside: N to S. Inside: S to N. This ensures field lines form complete closed loops.

(D) Magnetic field lines are NOT closed curves — INCORRECT statement ✗ (This is the answer):
Magnetic field lines ARE always closed curves (closed loops). They emerge from North pole, travel outside to South pole, then continue through the magnet interior from S back to N — forming a complete closed loop.

Key difference:
- Electric field lines: start at (+) charges, end at (-) charges → NOT closed
- Magnetic field lines: always form CLOSED LOOPS (no magnetic monopoles exist)

This is the fundamental reason statement (D) is NOT true.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Magnetic field line properties are fundamental to DRDO's electromagnetic systems design, MRI-safe military equipment, and magnetic anomaly detection systems for mine sweeping operations.

questions.text: If a person moves a trolley for a distance of 10 m with a force of 50 N, then the work done is:
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (D) 500 J

📚 NCERT: Class 9 Science | Chapter 11 — Work and Energy
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

Formula (force applied in direction of displacement, angle = 0°):
\( W = F \times d \)

\( W = 50 \text{ N} \times 10 \text{ m} = 500 \text{ J} \)

---

(A) 0.2 J — INCORRECT: This is \( d/F = 10/50 = 0.2 \). Wrong operation (division).

(B) 5 J — INCORRECT: This is \( F/d = 50/10 = 5 \). Wrong operation (division).

(C) 20 J — INCORRECT: No standard formula gives 20 from these values.

(D) 500 J — CORRECT ✓: \( W = 50 \times 10 = 500 \) J.

Units check: \( [W] = \text{N} \times \text{m} = \text{Joule (J)} \) ✓

General formula: \( W = F \times d \times \cos\theta \)
Here \( \theta = 0° \), so \( \cos 0° = 1 \), giving \( W = Fd \).

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Work calculation is used at ARDE to calculate the work done by propellant gases on a projectile inside the gun barrel — directly determining muzzle energy.

questions.text: What was the unknown element under aluminium called?
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (C) Eka-Aluminium

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 5 — Periodic Classification of Elements
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

Mendeleev's Eka-Element System:
When Mendeleev arranged elements by atomic mass (1869), he left gaps for undiscovered elements. He used the Sanskrit prefix 'Eka' (meaning 'one' / 'the next') to name predicted elements:

| Mendeleev's Prediction | Discovered As | Year |
|---|---|---|
| Eka-Aluminium | Gallium (Ga) | 1875 |
| Eka-Silicon | Germanium (Ge) | 1886 |
| Eka-Boron | Scandium (Sc) | 1879 |

Mendeleev predicted Eka-Aluminium's density (~6 g/cm³), atomic mass (~68), and oxide formula (Ga₂O₃) with remarkable accuracy — all confirmed when Gallium was discovered by Lecoq de Boisbaudran in 1875.

---

(A) Neo-Aluminium — INCORRECT: No such element prediction exists.

(B) Proto-Aluminium — INCORRECT: No such element prediction exists.

(C) Eka-Aluminium — CORRECT ✓: Mendeleev's prediction for the element below Aluminium, later identified as Gallium (Ga, Z=31).

(D) Meta-Aluminium — INCORRECT: 'Meta' prefix is used for ring compound positions, not element predictions.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Gallium (the 'Eka-Aluminium') is a critical material in DRDO's semiconductor research — GaAs and GaN are used in DRDO's high-frequency radar modules and electronic warfare systems.

questions.text: Which of the following laboratory safety equipment is used to extinguish small fires?
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (D) Fire extinguisher

📚 NCERT: Class 8/9 Science | General Lab Safety
⭐ Exam Relevance: Moderate

(A) Laboratory — INCORRECT: The room/facility itself, not a piece of safety equipment.

(B) Blanket (Fire Blanket) — INCORRECT as primary answer:
A fire blanket can smother small fires by cutting off oxygen. However, fire extinguisher is the standard, specifically designed fire-fighting equipment. Between these options, fire extinguisher is the correct answer.

(C) Safety goggles — INCORRECT:
Safety goggles protect the EYES from chemical splashes and flying particles. Personal protective equipment — not fire-fighting equipment.

(D) Fire extinguisher — CORRECT ✓:
Specifically designed to extinguish small fires. Types:
- CO₂ extinguisher: Electrical and chemical fires
- Dry powder extinguisher: General fires
- Water extinguisher: Paper/wood fires
- Foam extinguisher: Liquid fires

🔬 DRDO Relevance: CO₂ fire extinguishers are standard equipment in all DRDO electronics and chemical labs. Critical at HEMRL where explosive materials are handled.

questions.text:
Which of the following is an example of animal hormones?
I. Adrenalin
II. Testosterone
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (D) Both I and II

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 7 — Control and Coordination
⭐ Exam Relevance: High

I. Adrenalin (Epinephrine) — Animal hormone ✓
Secreted by: Adrenal medulla (above kidneys)
Function: 'Fight or flight' hormone — released during stress/fear. Increases heart rate, blood pressure, dilates pupils, raises blood glucose. Prepares body for emergency response.
Chemical nature: Catecholamine (derived from tyrosine)

II. Testosterone — Animal hormone ✓
Secreted by: Testes (in males)
Function: Primary male sex hormone — responsible for secondary sexual characteristics (voice deepening, facial hair, muscle development), sperm production.
Chemical nature: Steroid hormone

Both are ANIMAL hormones produced in animal endocrine glands.

---

(A) Only II — INCORRECT: Adrenalin is also an animal hormone.
(B) Only I — INCORRECT: Testosterone is also an animal hormone.
(C) Neither — INCORRECT: Both are well-established animal hormones.
(D) Both I and II — CORRECT ✓

🔬 DRDO Relevance: DRDO's DIPAS studies stress hormones including adrenalin in soldiers at high altitude and combat conditions. Testosterone research supports soldier fitness and performance programs.

questions.text:
Which of the following has disinfectant properties?
A. Amphoteric surfactant
B. Evaporative cleaner
C. Cationic detergent
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (A) Only C

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 4 — Carbon and its Compounds
⭐ Exam Relevance: Moderate

Types of surfactants/detergents by charge:

Cationic surfactants (Cationic detergents): Positively charged head — typically quaternary ammonium compounds (Quats).
Why they have DISINFECTANT properties:
- Positively charged head binds to negatively charged bacterial cell membranes
- Disrupts membrane integrity → kills bacteria
- Used in hospital disinfectants, sanitisers
Example: Benzalkonium chloride (BAC) — common hospital disinfectant.

Amphoteric surfactants: Both positive and negative charges (pH-dependent). Used in gentle cleansers and baby shampoos. Mild cleansing but NOT strong disinfectants.

Evaporative cleaners: Solvents (isopropyl alcohol, acetone) used for cleaning electronics. They clean by dissolving residues and evaporating — NOT classified as disinfectants in the detergent sense.

---

(A) Only C (Cationic detergent) — CORRECT ✓
(B) A and B — INCORRECT: Neither amphoteric surfactant nor evaporative cleaner are disinfectants.
(C) Only A — INCORRECT: Amphoteric surfactants lack strong disinfectant properties.
(D) A and C — INCORRECT: Only cationic detergents have disinfectant action.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Cationic disinfectants are used in DRDO's DRDE NBC decontamination protocols after biological agent exposure.

questions.text: Which of the following modern physicist closely examined Newtonian mechanics' foundations?
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (D) Albert Einstein

📚 NCERT: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 1 — Physical World
⭐ Exam Relevance: Moderate

Albert Einstein (1879–1955) most thoroughly and famously examined the foundations of Newtonian mechanics and showed where it breaks down:

1. Special Theory of Relativity (1905):
Newton assumed absolute space and absolute time. Einstein showed both are relative — they depend on the observer's motion. Time dilation and length contraction directly contradict Newtonian assumptions.
\( E = mc^2 \) — mass and energy are equivalent, unknown to Newton.

2. General Theory of Relativity (1915):
Newton's law of gravitation was replaced by Einstein's geometric theory of gravity — mass curves spacetime. Predictions: bending of light near massive objects, gravitational waves, black holes.

Einstein's work exposed the LIMITS of Newtonian mechanics (valid only at low speeds, weak gravity, large scales).

---

(A) James Clerk Maxwell — INCORRECT:
Maxwell formulated electromagnetism (Maxwell's equations). Worked within classical physics and did not directly examine Newton's mechanical foundations.

(B) Ernst Mach — NOT selected:
Mach did critique Newton's absolute space/time philosophically. However, Einstein is the more standard answer for a modern physicist who examined AND replaced Newtonian foundations scientifically.

(C) Galileo Galilei — INCORRECT:
Galileo PRECEDED Newton (1564-1642) and laid the groundwork FOR Newtonian mechanics. He is not a modern physicist.

(D) Albert Einstein — CORRECT ✓:
Einstein's Special and General Relativity directly examined, challenged, and replaced Newtonian mechanics' foundations. He is the definitive modern physicist who overthrew Newton.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Einstein's relativity is applied in GPS satellite timing corrections (without relativistic corrections, GPS would drift ~10 km/day) — critical for DRDO's precision-guided munitions that rely on GPS targeting.

questions.text: A sound wave with a period of 0.01 seconds has a frequency of:
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (D) 100 Hz

📚 NCERT: Class 9 Science | Chapter 12 — Sound
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

The relationship between frequency and time period:
\( f = \frac{1}{T} \)

Given: \( T = 0.01 \) seconds

\( f = \frac{1}{0.01} = \frac{1}{10^{-2}} = 10^2 = 100 \text{ Hz} \)

---

(A) 1000 Hz — INCORRECT: Would require \( T = 1/1000 = 0.001 \) s.

(B) 10 Hz — INCORRECT: Would require \( T = 1/10 = 0.1 \) s.

(C) 0.1 Hz — INCORRECT: Would require \( T = 1/0.1 = 10 \) s.

(D) 100 Hz — CORRECT ✓: \( f = 1/0.01 = 100 \) Hz. Falls in audible range (20-20,000 Hz) — a low bass sound.

Quick reference:
\( T = 0.001 \text{ s} \Rightarrow f = 1000 \text{ Hz} \)
\( T = 0.01 \text{ s} \Rightarrow f = 100 \text{ Hz} \)
\( T = 0.1 \text{ s} \Rightarrow f = 10 \text{ Hz} \)
\( T = 1 \text{ s} \Rightarrow f = 1 \text{ Hz} \)

🔬 DRDO Relevance: f = 1/T is fundamental to DRDO's SONAR, acoustic sensors, and all electronic signal processing in radar and communication systems.

questions.text: Which of the following is CORRECT regarding Ohm's law?
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (B) V ∝ I

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 12 — Electricity
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

Ohm's Law: At constant temperature, current through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference across it:

\( V \propto I \) (at constant R and temperature)

Or: \( V = IR \)

Where:
- V = potential difference (Volts)
- I = current (Amperes)
- R = resistance (Ohms) — the constant of proportionality

Rearranging: \( R = \frac{V}{I} \)

---

(A) R = V — INCORRECT: Dimensionally wrong. R in Ohms ≠ V in Volts.

(B) V ∝ I — CORRECT ✓: Direct statement of Ohm's Law. Voltage proportional to current at constant R.

(C) R ∝ I — INCORRECT: Resistance does NOT change with current (for ohmic conductors). R depends on material, geometry, and temperature — NOT current.

(D) V = I — INCORRECT: V and I have different units (Volts vs Amperes). This equation would require R = 1 Ω always — not generally true.

Graphically: V vs I gives a straight line through origin for ohmic conductors. Slope = R.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Ohm's Law is fundamental to all DRDO electronics design — radar systems, missile electronics, and electronic warfare equipment all require careful V-I analysis to ensure safe operation.

questions.text: ______ are those errors that tend to be in one direction, either positive or negative.
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (B) Systematic error

📚 NCERT: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 2 — Units and Measurements
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

Types of Errors:

(A) Random errors:
Errors varying unpredictably in magnitude AND direction from one measurement to the next. Can be positive or negative randomly.
Cause: Unpredictable fluctuations in experimental conditions.
Reduction: Statistical averaging over many measurements.

(B) Systematic errors — CORRECT ✓:
Errors consistently in ONE direction — always positive (overestimate) or always negative (underestimate).
Cause: Faulty instrument calibration, wrong technique, environmental factors.
Example: A balance that always reads 5 g MORE than true value → constant positive systematic error.
Reduction: Calibration of instruments, improved technique.

(C) Instrumental errors:
A SUBTYPE of systematic error from faulty/poorly calibrated instruments. More specific than systematic error.

(D) Personal errors:
Due to observer's limitations — parallax error, wrong digit estimation. Can be systematic or random.

The defining phrase 'tend to be in ONE direction' = systematic error.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Systematic error analysis is critical in DRDO's missile accuracy assessment — systematic errors in guidance cause consistent miss distances (CEP — Circular Error Probable) that must be corrected through calibration.

questions.text: In 1995, physicists Eric Cornell and Carl Wieman created the first Bose-Einstein condensate (BEC) using which isotope?
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (C) Rubidium-87

📚 NCERT: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 1 — Physical World
⭐ Exam Relevance: Moderate

Bose-Einstein Condensate (BEC):
A state of matter formed when bosons are cooled to temperatures approaching absolute zero:
\( T \approx 10^{-7} \text{ K (nano-Kelvin range)} \)

Particles lose individual identities and collectively occupy the same quantum ground state, behaving as a single quantum entity. Predicted by Satyendra Nath Bose and Albert Einstein in 1924-25.

First experimental BEC (1995):
- Scientists: Eric Cornell and Carl Wieman at JILA, University of Colorado
- Isotope: Rubidium-87 (⁸⁷Rb)
- Temperature: ~170 nanoKelvin
- Atoms used: ~2000 Rb-87 atoms
- Nobel Prize 2001: Cornell, Wieman, and Wolfgang Ketterle (MIT, used Na-23)

---

(A) Caesium-133 — INCORRECT: Used in atomic clocks. Not the first BEC.

(B) Thorium-232 — INCORRECT: Radioactive nuclear fuel. Not used in BEC experiments.

(C) Rubidium-87 — CORRECT ✓: Used by Cornell and Wieman for the FIRST BEC.

(D) Sodium-23 — INCORRECT: Used by Ketterle (MIT) slightly later in 1995. Often confused with the first BEC.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: BEC-based atom interferometers are being developed for ultra-precise inertial navigation without GPS — strategically important for DRDO's missile guidance applications.

questions.text: An alloy with ______ in it is referred to as 'amalgam'.
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (C) mercury

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 3 — Metals and Non-Metals
⭐ Exam Relevance: High

Amalgam = any alloy containing MERCURY (Hg) as one of its components.

Common amalgams:
1. Dental amalgam: Hg + Ag + Sn + Zn (~50% Hg) — dental fillings
2. Sodium amalgam (Na-Hg): reducing agent in chemical industry
3. Zinc amalgam (Zn-Hg): used in Clemmensen reduction
4. Gold amalgam: historically used in gold extraction from ore

Special property: Mercury is the ONLY metal liquid at room temperature (mp = -38.83°C), making it unique in forming liquid alloys with solid metals.

---

(A) Nickel — INCORRECT: Ni alloys = nichrome, cupronickel. Not amalgams.

(B) Silver — INCORRECT: Ag alloys = sterling silver. Silver is IN dental amalgam but mercury is the DEFINING element.

(C) Mercury — CORRECT ✓: Any alloy with mercury = amalgam.

(D) Cobalt — INCORRECT: Co alloys = cobalt steel, stellite. Not amalgams.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Mercury fulminate (Hg(CNO)₂) is a primary explosive used in detonators and blasting caps — a mercury compound central to DRDO's ordnance design at HEMRL.

questions.text: The potential difference between the terminals of an electric heater is 60 V when it draws a current of 4 A from the source. What current will the heater draw if the potential difference is changed to 127.5 V?
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (A) 8.5 A

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 12 — Electricity
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

Step 1: Find the resistance of the heater.
\( R = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{60 \text{ V}}{4 \text{ A}} = 15 \text{ Ω} \)

Step 2: Find the new current at 127.5 V (same heater, same resistance).
\( I = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{127.5}{15} = 8.5 \text{ A} \)

---

(A) 8.5 A — CORRECT ✓: \( R = 15\Omega \), then \( I = 127.5/15 = 8.5 \) A.

(B) 24 A — INCORRECT: No standard calculation from given values gives 24 A.

(C) 10 A — INCORRECT: Would require R = 12.75 Ω, not 15 Ω.

(D) 12 A — INCORRECT: Would require R = 127.5/12 = 10.625 Ω, not 15 Ω.

Verification:
Original power: \( P_1 = V_1 I_1 = 60 \times 4 = 240 \) W
New power: \( P_2 = V_2 I_2 = 127.5 \times 8.5 = 1083.75 \) W
Ratio: \( P_2/P_1 = (V_2/V_1)^2 = (127.5/60)^2 = 2.125^2 \approx 4.5 \) ✓

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Two-step Ohm's Law calculations are fundamental for DRDO's electrical engineering teams designing power systems for defence equipment — preventing damage to sensitive radar and missile electronics from voltage variations.

questions.text: Element X forms an oxide with the formula X2O3. The element X would most likely be in the same group of the periodic table as _________
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (C) aluminium

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 5 — Periodic Classification of Elements
⭐ Exam Relevance: High

Determining valency from oxide formula:

Formula \( X_2O_3 \):
- Oxygen has charge = -2 (fixed)
- For electrical neutrality: \( 2(+n) + 3(-2) = 0 \)
- \( 2n = 6 \Rightarrow n = +3 \)
- Element X has valency = +3

Checking each option's valency:
- Magnesium (Mg, Group 2): valency = +2 → forms MgO. NOT +3.
- Silicon (Si, Group 14): valency = +4 → forms SiO₂. NOT +3.
- Aluminium (Al, Group 13): valency = +3 → forms Al₂O₃. MATCHES ✓
- Sodium (Na, Group 1): valency = +1 → forms Na₂O. NOT +3.

Group 13 elements: B, Al, Ga, In, Tl — all have valency +3 and form \( X_2O_3 \) oxides.

X has valency +3 → X is in Group 13 → same group as Aluminium.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Aluminium alloys form the structural material of India's Agni and Prithvi missile bodies. Group 13 element Gallium is used in GaN and GaAs semiconductors for DRDO's radar and microwave systems.

questions.text: What is the work done when a constant force of 20 N is applied at an angle of 30 degrees to the direction of displacement of 13 m?
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (B) 225 Joules

📚 NCERT: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 6 — Work, Energy and Power
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

Formula for work done when force is at angle \( \theta \) to displacement:
\( W = F \times d \times \cos\theta \)

Given: F = 20 N, d = 13 m, \( \theta = 30° \)

\( \cos 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866 \)

\( W = 20 \times 13 \times \cos 30° \)
\( W = 260 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \)
\( W = 130\sqrt{3} \approx 130 \times 1.732 = 225.16 \approx 225 \text{ J} \)

---

(A) 390 J — INCORRECT: Uses \( \cos 30° \approx 1.5 \) (wrong — confuses \( 2/\sqrt{3} \) with \( \sqrt{3}/2 \)).

(B) 225 J — CORRECT ✓: \( W = 260 \times 0.866 \approx 225 \) J.

(C) 260 J — INCORRECT: This is \( 20 \times 13 = 260 \) — work done at 0° (ignores the angle entirely).

(D) 185 J — INCORRECT: No standard formula gives 185 J from these values.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Work done at an angle is used at ARDE for calculating effective horizontal work done by propellant forces on artillery projectiles when the barrel is elevated at an angle.

questions.text: The enzyme .............. present in the pancreatic juice, converts proteins to amino acids.
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (C) Trypsin

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 6 — Life Processes
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

⚠️ Note: The provided answer key listed (D) Amylase for this question. However, Amylase converts STARCH to maltose — it has no role in protein digestion. The scientifically correct NCERT answer is (C) Trypsin, which is the pancreatic enzyme specifically responsible for converting proteins to amino acids.

Digestive Enzyme Reference Table:

| Enzyme | Source | Substrate | Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| Salivary Amylase | Salivary glands | Starch | Maltose |
| Pepsin | Stomach | Proteins | Peptides |
| Trypsin | Pancreas | Proteins/Peptides | Amino acids |
| Lipase | Pancreas | Fats | Fatty acids + Glycerol |
| Pancreatic Amylase | Pancreas | Starch | Maltose |

---

(A) Pepsin — INCORRECT:
Pepsin is in GASTRIC juice (stomach), not pancreatic juice. Breaks proteins into peptides, not complete amino acids.

(B) Lipase — INCORRECT:
Lipase is in pancreatic juice but acts on FATS (lipids), not proteins. Converts fats to fatty acids + glycerol.

(C) Trypsin — CORRECT ✓:
Trypsin is secreted by the PANCREAS as inactive trypsinogen, activated in the small intestine. It hydrolyses proteins and peptides into amino acids. This is the correct NCERT answer.

(D) Amylase — INCORRECT:
Pancreatic amylase acts on STARCH (carbohydrates), converting it to maltose. Has NO role in protein digestion.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Enzyme biochemistry is studied by DRDO's DIPAS for soldier nutrition research and DIBER for enzyme-based biosensors for biological threat detection.

questions.text: Which of the following inert gases is placed in period 4 of the periodic table?
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (A) Kr (Krypton)

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 5 — Periodic Classification of Elements
⭐ Exam Relevance: High

Noble Gases (Group 18) and their Periods:

| Noble Gas | Symbol | Atomic Number | Period |
|---|---|---|---|
| Helium | He | 2 | 1 |
| Neon | Ne | 10 | 2 |
| Argon | Ar | 18 | 3 |
| Krypton | Kr | 36 | 4 |
| Xenon | Xe | 54 | 5 |
| Radon | Rn | 86 | 6 |

Period 4 noble gas = Krypton (Kr, Z = 36) ✓

---

(A) Kr (Krypton, Z = 36) — CORRECT ✓: Period 4.

(B) Rn (Radon, Z = 86) — INCORRECT: Period 6. Only radioactive noble gas.

(C) Xe (Xenon, Z = 54) — INCORRECT: Period 5. Used in ion propulsion engines.

(D) Ar (Argon, Z = 18) — INCORRECT: Period 3. Most abundant noble gas in atmosphere (~0.93%). Used in welding as inert shield gas.

Memory tip: Noble gas periods in sequence: He(1), Ne(2), Ar(3), Kr(4), Xe(5), Rn(6).

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Argon (Period 3) is used by DRDO's DMRL for inert atmosphere welding and processing of reactive metals in missile structures. Krypton is used in high-intensity lights for target illumination systems.

questions.text: Plants in the ............ group are commonly called algae.
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (B) Thallophyta

📚 NCERT: Class 9 Science | Chapter 7 — Diversity in Living Organisms
⭐ Exam Relevance: High

Plant Kingdom Classification (primitive to advanced):
Thallophyta → Bryophyta → Pteridophyta → Gymnosperms → Angiosperms

(A) Bryophyta — INCORRECT:
'Amphibians of the plant kingdom'. Includes mosses and liverworts. Small, non-vascular, need water for reproduction. NOT algae.

(B) Thallophyta — CORRECT ✓:
Most primitive plant division:
- Body = thallus (NOT differentiated into root, stem, leaf)
- Includes all ALGAE (green, red, brown algae) and fungi
- Examples: Spirogyra, Ulva, Chlorella
- Predominantly aquatic
- No vascular tissue

(C) Gymnosperms — INCORRECT:
Advanced seed-bearing plants with NAKED seeds. Examples: Pine, Cycas, Ginkgo. Complex vascular plants — NOT algae.

(D) Pteridophyta — INCORRECT:
Ferns and horsetails. First vascular plants (have xylem and phloem). Reproduce by spores. NOT algae.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: DRDO's DIBER studies algae as biofuel sources for defence applications, reducing military dependence on fossil fuels.

questions.text: Which of the following instruments is primarily used by the RBI for monetary policy implementation in the post 1991 reform era?
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (A) Repo rate

📚 NCERT: Class 12 Economics | Indian Economic Development
⭐ Exam Relevance: High

Repo Rate = rate at which RBI lends money to commercial banks against government securities.

- RBI INCREASES repo rate: Borrowing expensive → banks lend less → money supply decreases → inflation controlled
- RBI DECREASES repo rate: Borrowing cheap → banks lend more → money supply increases → growth stimulated

Post-1991 reform era shift:
- Pre-1991: Direct quantitative controls (CRR, SLR were primary)
- Post-1991: Price-based instrument — Repo rate became the PRIMARY monetary policy tool (Liquidity Adjustment Facility / LAF framework)

---

(A) Repo rate — CORRECT ✓: RBI's primary monetary policy instrument post-1991.

(B) Export incentives — INCORRECT: Trade policy instrument (Commerce Ministry), not RBI tool.

(C) Tariff rates — INCORRECT: Customs duties (Finance Ministry/trade policy), not monetary policy.

(D) Direct taxes — INCORRECT: Fiscal policy instrument (Finance Ministry/CBDT), not RBI.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Lower repo rates reduce government borrowing costs, potentially freeing budget resources for defence procurement and DRDO R&D.

questions.text: Calcium sulfate hemihydrate is also known as:
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (D) Plaster of Paris

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 2 — Acids, Bases and Salts
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

Important Calcium Compounds:

| Formula | Common Name |
|---|---|
| CaSO₄·2H₂O | Gypsum |
| CaSO₄·½H₂O | Plaster of Paris (POP) |
| CaO | Quicklime / Burnt lime |
| Ca(OH)₂ | Slaked lime |
| CaCO₃ | Limestone / Chalk / Marble |

Manufacture of POP by heating Gypsum:
\( 2(\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}) \xrightarrow{373\text{ K}} 2\left(\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}\right) + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \)

When POP is mixed with water, it expands and sets hard (reversing back to gypsum):
\( \text{CaSO}_4 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + \frac{3}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \)

---

(A) Baking powder — INCORRECT: NaHCO₃ + cream of tartar + starch. No calcium sulfate.

(B) Baking soda — INCORRECT: NaHCO₃ (Sodium hydrogen carbonate). Completely different.

(C) Washing soda — INCORRECT: Na₂CO₃·10H₂O (Sodium carbonate decahydrate).

(D) Plaster of Paris — CORRECT ✓: CaSO₄·½H₂O = Plaster of Paris.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: POP is used in DRDO manufacturing facilities for mould-making in prototype weapon component casting, and in military hospitals for orthopaedic casts.

questions.text: A compound has an empirical formula CH2 and a molar mass of 56 g/mol. Its molecular formula is:
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (D) C4H8

📚 NCERT: Class 11 Chemistry | Chapter 1 — Some Basic Concepts of Chemistry
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

Step 1: Molar mass of empirical formula (CH₂):
\( M_{empirical} = 12 + 2(1) = 14 \text{ g/mol} \)

Step 2: Find n (multiplication factor):
\( n = \frac{\text{Molar mass of compound}}{\text{Molar mass of empirical formula}} = \frac{56}{14} = 4 \)

Step 3: Molecular formula = empirical formula × n:
\( (\text{CH}_2)_4 = \text{C}_4\text{H}_8 \)

Verification: Molar mass of C₄H₈ = 4(12) + 8(1) = 48 + 8 = 56 g/mol ✓

---

(A) C₂H₄ — INCORRECT: Molar mass = 28 g/mol (n=2, not 4).

(B) C₃H₆ — INCORRECT: Molar mass = 42 g/mol (n=3, not 4).

(C) CH₂ — INCORRECT: This IS the empirical formula (14 g/mol), not the molecular formula.

(D) C₄H₈ — CORRECT ✓: Molar mass = 56 g/mol, n = 4. Examples: 1-butene, 2-butene, cyclobutane — all C₄H₈.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Empirical-to-molecular formula conversion is used at HEMRL to accurately determine composition of new energetic materials and propellant compounds.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly represents the chronological order of human development during gestation?
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (C) Zygote -> Embryo -> Fetus

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 8 — How Do Organisms Reproduce?
⭐ Exam Relevance: High

Chronological stages of human prenatal development:

Stage 1 — Zygote (Week 0-1):
Formed immediately after fertilisation (sperm + egg). Single cell with 46 chromosomes. Undergoes rapid cell division (cleavage) travelling down the fallopian tube.

Stage 2 — Embryo (Week 2 to Week 8):
After zygote implants in uterine wall, called an embryo. All major organs begin forming (organogenesis). Heart, brain, limbs, sensory organs develop. Most critical period for development.

Stage 3 — Fetus (Week 9 to Birth ~40 weeks):
From week 9 onward = fetus. Organs mature and grow. Movement begins (quickening). Grows from ~3 cm to ~50 cm.

Correct order: Zygote → Embryo → Fetus ✓

---

(A) Embryo → Zygote → Fetus — INCORRECT: Zygote forms BEFORE embryo.
(B) Fetus → Embryo → Zygote — INCORRECT: Exactly reversed.
(C) Zygote → Embryo → Fetus — CORRECT ✓
(D) Zygote → Fetus → Embryo — INCORRECT: Embryo stage precedes fetus stage.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: DRDO's DIPAS studies foetal physiology for understanding effects of extreme environments on pregnancies of military personnel families.

questions.text: For the case of a curved mirror, the pole, centre of curvature and the principal focus are connected by the:
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (B) principal axis

📚 NCERT: Class 10 Science | Chapter 10 — Light: Reflection and Refraction
⭐ Exam Relevance: Very High

Key definitions for a spherical (curved) mirror:

Pole (P): Geometric centre of the reflecting surface.

Centre of Curvature (C): Centre of the sphere from which the mirror is a part. At distance R from pole.

Principal Focus (F): Point where parallel rays converge (concave) or appear to diverge from (convex) after reflection. At distance f = R/2 from pole.

Principal Axis: Straight line passing through ALL three — Pole (P), Centre of Curvature (C), and Principal Focus (F).

\( f = \frac{R}{2} \) — both measured along the PRINCIPAL AXIS.

---

(A) Lens axis — INCORRECT: Non-standard terminology for mirrors.

(B) Principal axis — CORRECT ✓: P, C, and F all lie on the principal axis.

(C) Radius of curvature — INCORRECT: R is a length (distance from P to C), not a line connecting all three points.

(D) Mirror axis — INCORRECT: Not standard optical terminology.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Principal axis optics are fundamental in DRDO's IRDE for designing searchlights, laser collimators, and thermal imaging systems.

questions.text: Which statement about lyophilic sols is correct?
options: They do not show the Tyndall effect.
They are easily coagulated by electrolytes.
They are unstable compared to lyophobic sols.
They have strong affinity for the dispersion medium.
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (D) They have strong affinity for the dispersion medium.

📚 NCERT: Class 12 Chemistry | Chapter 5 — Surface Chemistry
⭐ Exam Relevance: High

Lyophilic vs Lyophobic Sols:
'Lyophilic' = solvent-loving. 'Lyophobic' = solvent-hating.

| Property | Lyophilic | Lyophobic |
|---|---|---|
| Affinity for medium | STRONG | Weak |
| Stability | MORE stable | Less stable |
| Tyndall effect | Shows (less prominently) | Shows (more prominently) |
| Coagulation | Difficult (needs large electrolyte) | Easy (small electrolyte enough) |
| Examples | Starch, gelatin, gum, protein | Gold sol, As₂S₃ sol |

---

(A) They do not show Tyndall effect — INCORRECT:
All colloids (including lyophilic sols) show the Tyndall effect (scattering of light by colloidal particles).

(B) They are easily coagulated by electrolytes — INCORRECT:
This is a property of LYOPHOBIC sols. Lyophilic sols RESIST coagulation — they need large amounts of electrolyte to coagulate (salting out).

(C) They are unstable compared to lyophobic — INCORRECT:
Lyophilic sols are MORE STABLE than lyophobic sols due to their strong solvation layer.

(D) They have strong affinity for the dispersion medium — CORRECT ✓:
This is the defining characteristic of lyophilic sols — strong interaction with solvent molecules makes them more stable, viscous, and coagulation-resistant.

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Colloid chemistry is relevant at DRDE for studying aerosol-based smoke screens and chemical agents, and at HEMRL for colloidal stability of energetic material slurries.

questions.text: A tank, filled partially with a liquid, is subjected to a uniform horizontal acceleration. Which of the following is true for the surface of liquid in the tank?
options: The liquid surface falls down on the direction of motion and rises up on the back side of the tank
The liquid surface falls down only at the center of the tank
The surface of the liquid remains horizontal
The liquid surface rises up on the direction of motion and falls down on the back side of the tank
questions.solution:
✅ Correct Answer: (A) The liquid surface falls down on the direction of motion and rises up on the back side of the tank

📚 NCERT: Class 11 Physics | Chapter 10 — Mechanical Properties of Fluids
⭐ Exam Relevance: High

Physics of liquid in a horizontally accelerating tank:

When the tank accelerates forward (say, to the right), the liquid has INERTIA — it tends to resist the change in motion and 'stay behind'.

In the non-inertial reference frame of the tank, a pseudo-force acts BACKWARD on every liquid particle:
\( F_{pseudo} = -ma \text{ (backward, opposite to acceleration)} \)

This backward pseudo-force pushes liquid toward the REAR of the tank:
- Liquid level at BACK = RISES
- Liquid level at FRONT (direction of motion) = FALLS

The free surface tilts backward at angle \( \theta \) where:
\( \tan\theta = \frac{a}{g} \)

This is identical to the observation in everyday life: when a car accelerates forward, liquid in a glass tilts backward (front falls, back rises).

---

(A) Surface falls at front, rises at back — CORRECT ✓:
Liquid 'lags behind' due to inertia → accumulates at rear → back rises, front falls.

(B) Falls only at center — INCORRECT: No physical reason for this. The surface tilts uniformly, not just at the centre.

(C) Surface remains horizontal — INCORRECT: This is true only at constant velocity (no acceleration). With acceleration, the surface tilts.

(D) Rises at front, falls at back — INCORRECT: This is the opposite and physically wrong for forward acceleration. (This would occur during braking/deceleration.)

🔬 DRDO Relevance: Fuel sloshing in missile and aircraft tanks during acceleration is a critical engineering problem for DRDO's DRDL — sloshing changes the centre of mass and can cause guidance errors in Agni and BrahMos programs.
